title: Race and the Chilean Miracle: Neoliberalism, Democracy, and Indigenous Rights By Patricia Richards University of Pittsburgh Press. 2013. 261 pages. $26.95 paper
abstract: In the aftermath of its peaceful transition from dictatorship, Chile has been lifted up as a model by those who say market reforms prepared it for global capitalism and multicultural democracy. While the “Chilean Miracle” is often attributed to neoliberal reforms, sociologist Patricia Richards challenges the association between neoliberalism and democracy by examining the experiences of the Mapuche, one of the last-conquered large sovereign indigenous nations in the Americas. Her ethnographic research shows how neoliberalism bolstered decades of colonial oppression and entrenched systemic racism, leaving the Mapuche in a Catch-22 that makes it hard for them to gain autonomous voice and make demands on the state without being suspected of inauthenticity or, worse, terrorism. The strength of her book lies in how Richards resists the common pattern of “eliding” race as part of the story of Chile's transition to democracy by looking in a detailed way at both state policy and local subjectivities. Doing both is a juggling act, but Richards pulls it off with aplomb.
